content:
Formal study of **outliers** grew from **descriptive statistics** and **exploratory data analysis** in the 20th century. **John Tukey** popularized the **box plot** and **IQR fences** (1.5×IQR rule) as simple, distribution-free tools for spotting unusual values in skewed real-world measurements.

Classical **robust statistics** (median, **M-estimators**, **Huber** 1964) developed parallel tracks: instead of deleting extremes, estimators were designed to limit their influence on means and regressions.

With the rise of **least squares** regression, **Anscombe's quartet** and **leverage** diagnostics showed that a few points could dominate a fitted line—motivating **winsorization** in econometrics and **RANSAC** (1981) for computer vision.

Modern ML pipelines treat outlier handling as part of reproducible **feature engineering**, often embedded in sklearn **Pipelines** alongside scaling and imputation, with the same train-only fitting discipline used across preprocessing.

timeline.label: Tukey and EDA
timeline.description: Box plots and IQR fences give practitioners robust, visual outlier rules without assuming normality.

timeline.label: Robust estimation
timeline.description: Huber and others introduce losses and estimators that resist contamination by a fraction of bad points.

timeline.label: ML pipelines
timeline.description: Clip, remove, and robust-model choices are versioned with estimators to prevent leakage and silent notebook drift.
